patches.find: What the dome predicts: −17 to −21 nT Z-component anomaly at European INTERMAGNET stations, conditional on Kp < 2.
patches.replace: What the dome predicts: Per-station Z-component anomalies at 9 European INTERMAGNET stations, computed from δZ = baseline × coverage × FSF, where FSF is a station-specific "field strength factor" derived from the dome's H(r)/r geometry. Two baselines are used: the BOU 2017 eclipse observation (−10.9 nT) and an empirical "W004" value (−22.24 nT), producing dual prediction ranges from −5.0 nT (Canary Islands, BOU baseline) to −26.2 nT (Hartland, W004 baseline). All conditional on Kp < 2.
patches.rationale: The dome no longer predicts a simple -17 to -21 nT range. V51.0 uses a 9-station dual-baseline system with station-specific FSF values (0.642-2.075). Our section was attacking a simplified version that no longer exists.

patches.find: How the dome prediction was actually derived: The author took real INTERMAGNET data from the March 9, 2016 Pacific eclipse (station GUA: −13.16 to −15.11 nT on a quiet day), applied his "correction factor of 1.672," and arrived at −17 to −21 nT. Those 2016 observations are Chapman-mechanism data recorded on a spherical Earth. He is scaling globe-confirmed measurements slightly upward and calling them a dome prediction.
patches.replace: How the dome prediction was actually derived: The formula is δZ = baseline × coverage × FSF. The two baselines are both empirical: BOU is the −10.9 nT signal observed at Boulder during the 2017 eclipse; W004 is −22.24 nT from an empirical fit. The coverage fraction is the local solar obscuration at each station (0.40–0.95). The FSF (field strength factor, 0.642–2.075) varies by station and is claimed to derive from dome geometry via H(r)/r. However, both baselines are themselves Chapman-mechanism observations recorded on a spherical Earth — the dome is scaling globe-confirmed measurements by dome-geometric factors and calling the result a dome prediction. (An earlier version of this section incorrectly stated the dome "applied his correction factor of 1.672" — that value is κ, the mag-gravity coupling constant from WIN-012, not the eclipse FSF.)
patches.rationale: The 1.672 factor is KAPPA (WIN-012 coupling constant), not the eclipse FSF. The dome uses station-specific FSF values (0.642-2.075) derived from H(r)/r. Factual error in our text.

patches.find: 3. No unique signature: A genuinely discriminating test would require the dome to predict something the Chapman mechanism cannot — for example, a directional pattern following "aetheric pressure" geometry rather than ionospheric conductivity geometry. The dome predicts the same phenomenon, from the same data, in the same range.
patches.replace:
3. No unique signature: A genuinely discriminating test would require the dome to predict something the Chapman mechanism cannot — for example, a directional pattern following "aetheric pressure" geometry rather than ionospheric conductivity geometry. The dome predicts the same phenomenon, from the same data, in the same range.

The dome's strongest sub-prediction (E-PRED-B): The dome's registered predictions include E-PRED-B: "Hartland beats Ebro despite lower eclipse coverage." Hartland (HAD, England) sees only 80% solar obscuration compared to Ebro's 95%, yet the dome predicts a larger magnetic anomaly at Hartland (−26.2 nT W004 baseline vs. Ebro's −21.7 nT) because Hartland's FSF (1.471) exceeds Ebro's (1.029). This is the dome's most interesting prediction because it is a relative ordering that does not depend on the absolute baseline choice — both baselines produce HAD > EBR. However, the FSF derives from the dome's H(r)/r function — the same exponential firmament formula that produces self-contradictions elsewhere (see Section 4.5). More importantly, the Chapman mechanism also predicts station-specific amplitudes based on ionospheric conductivity structure, local geomagnetic field geometry, and eclipse shadow dynamics — the relative ordering of stations is not unique to dome geometry. Whether Hartland exceeds Ebro will be testable on August 12, 2026, but the outcome alone will not discriminate between models: both have mechanisms that predict station-dependent amplitudes.
patches.rationale: E-PRED-B (Hartland > Ebro despite lower coverage) is the dome's strongest eclipse sub-prediction and was completely absent from our analysis. This is a relative prediction derived from dome geometry that we must engage with to maintain credibility.

patches.find: 117 Hz is the acoustic resonance frequency of the King's Chamber sarcophagus, documented by John Stuart Reid's cymatics experiments (1996–1997). This is an acoustic eigenmode determined by the sarcophagus geometry and the speed of sound in granite (~5,800–6,000 m/s) — standard Helmholtz resonance with no electromagnetic component.
patches.replace: 117 Hz is the acoustic resonance frequency of the King's Chamber sarcophagus, documented by John Stuart Reid's cymatics experiments (1996–1997). Reid's work reports multiple frequencies: the King's Chamber room resonance at ~125 Hz, a refined chamber measurement at ~121 Hz, and the granite sarcophagus resonance at ~117 Hz. The dome model selects 117 Hz without noting these alternatives. All three are acoustic eigenmodes determined by chamber/sarcophagus geometry and the speed of sound in granite (~5,800–6,000 m/s) — standard Helmholtz resonance with no electromagnetic component.
patches.rationale: WIN-003 picks 117 Hz without noting Reid's other measurements (125 Hz, 121 Hz). This selective citation should be documented to strengthen the 'post-hoc pattern match' argument.

patches.find: Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations were predicted by Chapman (1933): the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering conductivity, which alters the Sq current system.
patches.replace: Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations were first described by Chapman (1933), who proposed that the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering conductivity and altering the Sq current system. Chapman's original work was semi-quantitative; detailed magnitude predictions came from later studies and modern ionospheric models.
patches.rationale: Chapman (1933) was qualitative/semi-quantitative, not a precise magnitude prediction. Our text overstated the specificity of the 1933 paper. A dome defender could correctly note this overstatement.

patches.find: NOAA officially states no evidence of anomalous disappearances. does not charge premium rates for the region. U.S. Coast Guard reviews found no unusual causes.
patches.replace: NOAA officially states no evidence of anomalous disappearances. Lloyd's of London does not charge premium rates for the region. U.S. Coast Guard reviews found no unusual causes. The dome's claim has two parts: (1) the regions are disappearance zones, and (2) the Bermuda and Japan anomaly zones show 180° geomagnetic symmetry as predicted by dome geometry. Part (1) is debunked above. Part (2) conflates geographic folklore with geophysics — the Bermuda region is not a geomagnetic anomaly (the South Atlantic Anomaly, the actual major geomagnetic feature, is centered off the coast of Brazil, not Bermuda). The Japan region ("Devil's Sea") likewise has no distinctive geomagnetic signature. The dome's claimed 180° symmetry does not correspond to any pattern in IGRF or CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field models.
patches.rationale: The rebuttal was shallow (NOAA/Lloyd's debunking only) and didn't address the dome's specific geometric/geomagnetic symmetry claim. Also fixed missing 'Lloyd's of London' subject in sentence.

patches.find: The 2.66 area ratio is a numerical consequence of these known orbital parameters and requires no additional physical mechanism. No causal link between this ratio and dome dynamics has been proposed.
patches.replace: The dome claims a 2.66 lobe area ratio but provides no derivation from dome geometry — the value appears to be a post-hoc measurement of the analemma's known asymmetric lobe structure. The asymmetry arises from Earth's orbital eccentricity (faster motion near perihelion produces the larger lobe). The actual lobe area ratio depends on the measurement methodology and integration boundaries; published analyses of the equation of time confirm the asymmetry is fully explained by Keplerian orbital mechanics. No causal link between this ratio and dome dynamics has been proposed, and the dome provides no mechanism by which its flat-disc geometry would produce this specific value.
patches.rationale: We called 2.66 a 'coincidence' without verifying the value or explaining why it arises from standard orbital mechanics. Updated to explain the source of the asymmetry and note the dome offers no derivation.
